message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 27 (14 todo)
Recent changes: Added reimbursementFor helper that matches damage to insured item by type and applies 50% reimbursement when enchantment >= 8, else full; deductible subtracted after. Threaded policy into payoutFor.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion. Keep all tests green. Note: next claim test adds the dragon-material clause (full reimbursement); after that, a test where both clauses apply (50% wins).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Now let me run the tests to confirm the current state is all green before refactoring.

content.text:
All 27 tests pass. Now I'll run the Refactor phase analysis on the recently changed code (the `reimbursementFor` helper and `payoutFor` threading).

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Let me evaluate the names introduced in the recent Green phase.

**`reimbursementFor`**: Computes the covered amount for a single damage before deductible, applying the 50% high-enchantment rule.
- Current name: `reimbursementFor(damage, policy)`
- Purpose: returns the reimbursable amount for one damage entry, applying clause rules but NOT the deductible.
- Question: Does "reimbursementFor" reveal that the deductible is excluded? It is slightly ambiguous — "reimbursement" could imply the final paid amount. But within `payoutFor`, the deductible is subtracted separately, so `reimbursementFor` reading as "the covered value before deductible" is defensible.
- Assessment: Acceptable. The name reads naturally with its argument: `reimbursementFor(damage, policy)`. The upcoming dragon-material clause is also a reimbursement rule, so this name will continue to fit. **Keep.**

**`HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD` / `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE`**: Clear, distinguished from the quote-side enchantment constants by the `CLAIM`/`REIMBURSEMENT` qualifiers. **Keep.**

Naming is sound. No rename needed.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

Focusing on the recently-changed functions `reimbursementFor` and `payoutFor`.

```
function reimbursementFor(damage, policy):
- Bindings: damage, policy, item params/refs → item(1), damage(2 uses count once conceptually) ~ damage, policy, item = 3
- Invocations: policy.items.find (2), arrow (.find callback invocation counted as the find), item.type access, damage.amount, ?? operator
- Conditional: if (item && ...) = 4, plus && and ?? 
```

Rather than over-count, I'll use the rubric consistently:

**`reimbursementFor` (current):**
- Constants: `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD` ref, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE` ref, `0` (in `?? 0`) → treat the two named constant refs + literal 0 = 3
- Bindings: `damage`, `policy`, `item` = 3
- Invocations: `.find()` (2), `*` multiply (2) = 4
- Conditionals: `if` (4), `&&` (4) = 8
- Loops: 0
- Assignments: 0
- **Total: 3 + 3 + 4 + 8 = 18**

**`payoutFor` (current):**
- Bindings: `incident`, `policy`, `payout`, `damage` = 4
- Invocations: `reimbursementFor()` (2) = 2
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: `for...of` (5) = 5
- Assignments: `payout = 0` (6), `payout +=` (6) = 12
- **Total: 4 + 2 + 5 + 12 = 23**

Combined relevant mass: **41**.

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 — Tests Pass:** All 27 green. Confirmed.

**Rule 2 — Reveals Intent:** Names are clear. One small observation: `reimbursementFor` uses a shadowed parameter name `item` in the `.find` callback (`policy.items.find((item) => item.type === damage.itemType)`). The outer `const item` shadows the callback's `item`. This is legal but the shadowing slightly muddies reading. Minor.

**Rule 3 — No Duplication:** `payoutFor` and `capFor` both iterate `policy.items` / `incident.damages` with a manual accumulator loop (`let x = 0; for (...) x += ...; return x`). This is a repeated reduce-over-collection pattern. There is conceptual duplication of the imperative-accumulate idiom across `payoutFor`, `capFor`, and even `policyBasePremiumFor` and `surchargeFor` on the quote side. However, extracting a generic accumulator helper would hurt clarity (Rule 2 trumps Rule 3) and add elements (Rule 4). Not worth it.

**Rule 4 — Fewest Elements:** No unnecessary abstractions.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

The strongest improvement here, looking ahead to the dragon-material clause and the "both clauses → 50% wins" tests, is in `reimbursementFor`. The current structure computes a rate via a branch that returns early. The upcoming clauses (dragon = full/100%, high-enchantment = 50%, and when both apply the *lower* reimbursement wins) are most naturally expressed as **a set of applicable rate rules, taking the minimum rate**. But that's anticipating future tests — TDD forbids implementing ahead.

For now, I'll make a small, behavior-preserving clarity improvement: eliminate the variable shadowing of `item` and express the high-enchantment test through a focused predicate, making the single-clause structure read cleanly. This is a Rule 2 improvement.

content.text:
All 27 tests still green.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**`isHighlyEnchanted` (new):**
- Constants: `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD` ref, `0` = 2
- Bindings: `item` = 1
- Invocations: 0 (only property access + comparison)
- Conditionals: `?.` optional chain (4), `??` (4) — counting both branching operators = 8
- **Total: 2 + 1 + 8 = 11**

**`reimbursementFor` (refactored):**
- Constants: `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE` ref = 1
- Bindings: `damage`, `policy`, `insuredItem` = 3
- Invocations: `.find()` (2), `isHighlyEnchanted()` (2), `*` (2) = 6
- Conditionals: `if` (4) = 4 — the `&&` and `?? 0` branching moved into `isHighlyEnchanted`
- **Total: 1 + 3 + 6 + 4 = 14**

**`reimbursementFor` + `isHighlyEnchanted` combined: 25** vs. previous `reimbursementFor` **18**.

`payoutFor` unchanged at **23**.

**Mass Change:** The clause-evaluation portion went from 18 → 25 (Δ +7). Mass *increased* slightly because extracting the named predicate adds an invocation (2) and re-states the binding. This is the expected, accepted trade-off: **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP.** The `&&`/`?? 0` branching that previously sat inline in a compound conditional now has a clear name (`isHighlyEnchanted`) and the misleading variable shadow (`item` shadowing `item`) is gone (`insuredItem` for the outer binding, `item` only in the predicate). The clause check now reads as a sentence.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Extracted `isHighlyEnchanted(item)` predicate, naming the previously-inline branching condition `(item.enchantment ?? 0) >= THRESHOLD`. The reimbursement decision now reads `if (isHighlyEnchanted(insuredItem))`.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Renamed the shadowing outer binding `item` → `insuredItem`, so the `.find` callback's `item` no longer collides with the result variable.
- APP: clause-evaluation mass 18 → 25 (Δ +7), accepted because clarity trumps mass and the predicate is reusable for the upcoming dragon-material / "50%-wins" clauses.

**Why not more:**
- Rule 3 (Duplication): The accumulate-loop idiom recurs in `payoutFor`/`capFor`, but extracting a generic reducer would reduce clarity (Rule 2) and add elements (Rule 4) for no demonstrated need. Deferred.
- I deliberately did not restructure `reimbursementFor` into a "minimum-rate-over-applicable-clauses" form, even though the next tests point that way — that would implement ahead of the tests, violating TDD discipline.

## Step 7: Report Completion

🔄 **Refactor Phase Complete:**
- **Refactoring:** Extracted `isHighlyEnchanted` predicate and renamed shadowing binding `item` → `insuredItem` (Rule 2: Reveals Intent).
- **Mass Change:** clause-evaluation 18 → 25 (Δ +7), accepted — clarity trumps APP, predicate reused by upcoming clauses.
- **Tests:** All 27 passing ✅ (14 todo remain).

Proceeding to the next test (dragon-material clause: full reimbursement).

**Relevant files:**
- Implementation: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_06-12-03_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
- Spec: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_06-12-03_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`

**Note for the next Red phase:** The next two pending claim tests (`it.todo` lines 162–164) introduce the dragon-material clause (full reimbursement) and the "both clauses apply → 50% wins" case. When that logic lands in Green, `reimbursementFor` will likely want to become a "minimum reimbursement rate across applicable clauses" structure — the freshly-extracted `isHighlyEnchanted` predicate is positioned to support that.
